perturbation.original_text: Within one hundred twenty (120) days after delivery of the Software, Corio shall test the Software for conformance with the Documentation ("Acceptance Test"). If the Software performs in substantial accordance with the Documentation, then Corio shall notify Changepoint in writing of its acceptance of the Software. In the event Corio finds material errors or defects with the Software, Corio shall notify Changepoint in writing of such errors or defects and provide adequate detail to facilitate Changepoint replicating the error or defect. Upon receipt of written notice, Changepoint shall have fifteen (15) days to correct the defect, reinstall the Software at the Corio site and re-perform the Acceptance Test.
perturbation.changed_text: Within one hundred eighty (180) days after delivery of the Software, Corio shall test the Software for conformance with the Documentation ("Acceptance Test"). If the Software performs in substantial accordance with the Documentation, then Corio shall notify Changepoint in writing of its acceptance of the Software. In the event Corio finds material errors or defects with the Software, Corio shall notify Changepoint in writing of such errors or defects and provide adequate detail to facilitate Changepoint replicating the error or defect. Upon receipt of written notice, Changepoint shall have ten (10) days to correct the defect, reinstall the Software at the Corio site and re-perform the Acceptance Test.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the correction period to 10 days creates a legal contradiction. While general contract law doesn't mandate a specific time for remediation, setting an unreasonably short period (10 days) might violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially in complex software agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification changes the defect correction timeline to 10 days, which may be deemed unreasonably short for complex software defects, thus potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under California Commercial Code § 1201.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the defect correction timeline from 15 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation asserts that this shorter period might violate implied duties of good faith, especially for complex software agreements. The Law Explanation supports this, citing California Commercial Code § 1201 regarding the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 1201, defines terms related to agreements, but more importantly, it sets the stage for interpreting such agreements under California law. While the snippet itself doesn't explicitly define 'good faith,' the explanations highlight that an unreasonably short correction period can be interpreted as a breach of this duty. Given the complexity of software defects, reducing the correction time to 10 days could be deemed insufficient, leading to a potential failure to address issues adequately, thus contradicting the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any payment by Corio under this Agreement is not made within thirty (30) calendar days of its due date, interest on any such unpaid amount shall accrue at a rate of eighteen percent (18%) per annum, or the maximum amount permitted by law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any payment by Corio under this Agreement is not made within fifteen (15) calendar days of its due date, interest on any such unpaid amount shall accrue at a rate of twenty-five percent (25%) per annum, or the maximum amount permitted by law, whichever is less.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the interest rate to 25% and shortening the grace period to 15 days might violate usury laws, which cap the maximum legal interest rate that can be charged on a debt. Usury laws vary by state.
perturbation.law_explanation: Raising the interest rate to 25% directly contradicts California's usury laws (California Constitution Article XV Section 1) if that rate exceeds the allowable maximum for this type of transaction. The original text defers to this law, while the modified text actively defies it.

perturbation.justification: The modified text increases the interest rate to 25% and shortens the grace period to 15 days. The Law Explanation states that increasing the interest rate to 25% could directly contradict California's usury laws if it exceeds the allowable maximum. The original text defers to the law by stating "or the maximum amount permitted by law, whichever is less," while the modified text actively defies it by increasing the interest rate without regard for usury laws, if they exist. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces this by stating that increasing the interest rate and shortening the grace period might violate usury laws. The scraped law snippet is not helpful.

perturbation.original_text: Thereafter, this Agreement shall automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods unless either party provides the other party with written notification at least thirty (30) days prior to the expiration of the initial five (5) year term or any one (1) year renewal thereof of its intention to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Thereafter, this Agreement shall automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods unless either party provides the other party with written notification at least five (5) days prior to the expiration of the initial five (5) year term or any one (1) year renewal thereof of its intention to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period to 5 days may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient, particularly in agreements involving complex commercial relationships.  This may violate the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The original requires 30 days' notice to prevent automatic renewal. Reducing this to 5 days may be seen as a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as it provides inadequate time for the other party to prepare for the termination of the agreement, potentially harming their interests unfairly under California Commercial Code § 1201.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period from 30 days to 5 days, which introduces a potential violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation argues this is a violation because it provides inadequate time for the other party to prepare for the termination of the agreement, harming their interests. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "agreement" in California Commercial Code § 1201 as "the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303." The Perturbation Explanation notes that a 5-day notice period may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient, particularly in agreements involving complex commercial relationships, further supporting the argument that the altered text contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing within the contractual agreement.
